event: Natufian and early Neolithic communities settle the region, including the site of Jericho, one of the world's oldest continuously inhabited towns.
Israelite tribes emerge in the central highlands of Canaan during the Late Bronze Age collapse.
According to biblical tradition, King David establishes a united monarchy and makes Jerusalem his capital.
King Solomon builds the First Temple in Jerusalem, becoming the center of Israelite worship.
The united kingdom splits into the northern Kingdom of Israel and the southern Kingdom of Judah.
The Assyrian Empire conquers the northern Kingdom of Israel and deports much of its population.
The Babylonians under Nebuchadnezzar II destroy the First Temple and exile the people of Judah to Babylon.
Persian king Cyrus the Great allows Jewish exiles to return and rebuild Jerusalem.
The Second Temple is completed in Jerusalem, inaugurating the Second Temple period.
Alexander the Great conquers the region, bringing it under Hellenistic rule.
The Maccabean Revolt succeeds in rededicating the Temple, an event commemorated by Hanukkah.
The Hasmonean dynasty establishes an independent Jewish kingdom in Judea.
Roman general Pompey captures Jerusalem, bringing Judea under Roman influence.
Herod the Great becomes Roman client king of Judea and later greatly expands the Second Temple.
Rome annexes Judea as a province under direct imperial administration.
Roman forces under Titus destroy the Second Temple during the First Jewish-Roman War.
The Bar Kokhba revolt erupts against Roman rule, leading to renaming of the province as Syria Palaestina.
Muslim Arab armies under Caliph Umar capture Jerusalem, beginning centuries of Islamic rule.
The Dome of the Rock is completed in Jerusalem under the Umayyad Caliphate.
Crusaders capture Jerusalem and establish the Kingdom of Jerusalem.
Saladin defeats the Crusaders at the Battle of Hattin and retakes Jerusalem.
The Ottoman Empire conquers the region, ruling it for four centuries.
The First Aliyah begins as Jewish immigrants from Eastern Europe arrive to settle in Ottoman Palestine.
Theodor Herzl convenes the First Zionist Congress in Basel, founding the modern political Zionist movement.
Tel Aviv is founded as a modern Jewish town and the kibbutz Degania is established.
The British Balfour Declaration expresses support for a Jewish national home in Palestine.
The League of Nations grants Britain the Mandate for Palestine.
The UN General Assembly adopts Resolution 181, the plan to partition Palestine into Jewish and Arab states.
David Ben-Gurion declares the independence of the State of Israel, triggering the Arab-Israeli War.
Israel is admitted as a member of the United Nations.
The Knesset passes the Law of Return, granting Jews worldwide the right to immigrate to Israel.
Israel takes part in the Suez Crisis, invading the Sinai Peninsula alongside Britain and France.
Israel wins the Six-Day War, capturing the West Bank, Gaza, Sinai, the Golan Heights, and East Jerusalem.
Israel repels a surprise attack by Egypt and Syria in the Yom Kippur War.
Israel and Egypt sign a peace treaty following the Camp David Accords.
Israel invades Lebanon, beginning a prolonged military involvement there.
The First Intifada, a Palestinian uprising in the occupied territories, breaks out.
Israel and the PLO sign the Oslo Accords, establishing the Palestinian Authority.
Israel and Jordan sign a peace treaty, normalizing relations between the two countries.
Prime Minister Yitzhak Rabin is assassinated by a right-wing extremist in Tel Aviv.
Israel withdraws its forces from southern Lebanon and the Second Intifada erupts.
Israel unilaterally disengages from the Gaza Strip, evacuating its settlements there.
Israel fights the Second Lebanon War against Hezbollah.
Israel signs the Abraham Accords, normalizing relations with the UAE and Bahrain.
Hamas launches a large-scale surprise attack on October 7, triggering a major war in Gaza.
